entries.title: User Guide
entries.content:
User Guide

Welcome to the CrowdVision User Guide.

CrowdVision is a digital twin and crowd-analytics platform. It helps you monitor building occupancy, environmental conditions, and safety alerts in real time — from a single office floor to an entire campus.



Figure 1 — The dashboard you reach after signing in.

-----

What you can do

3D Digital TwinAnalytics DashboardSmart NotificationsData ManagementCapabilityA live, interactive 3D replica of your buildings and rooms.Real-time tables and charts of occupancy and environmental data.Instant alerts for overcrowding or high temperature, in-app and as browser push.Tools to manage domains, buildings, and room data.What it gives you

-----

Who it is for

- Facility managers — a bird’s-eye view of building health, with rapid response to alerts.
- Administrators — manage access, configure domains, and maintain the digital twin.
- General users — check room availability and stay informed about the spaces they use.

Account access
-----
Some views can be browsed as a guest, but actions such as editing rooms or managing domains require a registered account with the right role.

-----

How to use this guide

The guide follows the order you will naturally use the product: create an account, sign in, then explore each view. Use the navigation on the left to jump to any topic.



About the figures
-----
Every image in this guide is currently a placeholder that describes the screen it represents. To replace one with a real screenshot, drop your image into documentation/user/assets/figures/ using the file name shown in the placeholder and recompile. See assets/README.md for the full shot list.

entries.title: Account Registration
entries.content:
Account Registration

There are three ways to get a CrowdVision account. This page covers each. The diagram below helps you pick the right one.



-----

Standard account

A standard account lets you explore public domains and view building digital twins.

1. Open the CrowdVision application and open the Login / Register menu.
2. Choose Sign Up (Create an account).
3. Fill in the fields:
	- Username — a unique name for your profile.
	- Email — a valid email address.
	- Password — a secure password.
4. Leave the OTP field empty.
5. Click Sign Up. You can now sign in with your new credentials.



Figure 1 — The sign-up form. Leave OTP blank for a standard account.

-----

Account with an OTP (joining an organization)

If you are joining an existing organization (for example as staff), register with a one-time code. This grants the correct role and links you to the organization’s domain immediately.

1. Get an invite QR code from your organization’s domain administrator.
2. Scan the code with an authenticator app (Google Authenticator, Authy, FreeOTP). The app starts generating a six-digit code.
3. Open the CrowdVision registration page.
4. Enter your Username, Email, and Password.
5. Type the current six-digit code from your authenticator app into the OTP field.
6. Click Sign Up. Your account is created with the role and domain access tied to that code.



Figure 2 — Registering with an invite code in the OTP field.

Keep your authenticator app
-----
Keep the authenticator entry — you will use it again if your organization enforces two-factor sign-in.

-----

Business account (organizations)

Organizations manage their own buildings through isolated domains. Because domains need custom setup, business accounts are not created through the public sign-up page.

1. Contact CrowdVision support to request a new business domain.
2. Provide your organization details — name, desired domain (for example university.edu), and whether it should be public or private.
3. Setup and handoff — CrowdVision creates your domain and your root administrator account.
4. Manage your domain — sign in, open the Administrator Panel, upload your buildings, and generate invite QR codes for your staff (the OTP method above).

Next: sign in to your account.

headings.text: Account with an OTP (joining an organization)

entries.title: Account Login
entries.content:
Account Login

Signing in

1. Click the Login button in the top navigation bar.
2. The login dialog appears.
3. Enter your Username.
4. Enter your Password.
5. Click Login to access your account.



Figure 1 — The login dialog. Enter your username and password, then click Login.

Enterprise sign-in
-----
If your organization uses Single Sign-On, you sign in through your company identity provider instead. See Enterprise Login (SSO).

-----

Locked navigation

To protect your data, the core areas — Dashboard, Digital Twin, Domains, and the Administrator Panel — stay locked until you are signed in.

You do not have to click Login first. If you open any locked area while signed out, a Sign in to continue popup appears. Enter your credentials there, and once you are signed in the application unlocks and takes you straight to where you were headed.



Figure 2 — Opening a locked area while signed out prompts you to sign in, then continues to your destination.

entries.title: Enterprise Login (SSO)
entries.content:
Enterprise Login (SSO)

Some organizations sign in through their own identity provider (for example Keycloak, Microsoft Entra ID, or Auth0) using Single Sign-On (SSO). If your organization uses SSO, you do not set a separate CrowdVision password — you authenticate with your company account.

You need a CrowdVision account first
-----
SSO links your company identity to an existing CrowdVision account; it does not create one. If you have never signed in, create a standard account first (see Account Registration, then complete the SSO link below.

-----

How it works



-----

Steps

1. Sign in to CrowdVision (or create a standard account) and open the Domains view.
2. Choose your organization’s domain and start the SSO sign-in.
3. You are redirected to your company’s login page.
4. Authenticate with your usual company credentials.
5. You are returned to CrowdVision. Your membership and role are set automatically based on your company group, and the domain now appears in your Domains list.



Figure 1 — Starting the SSO sign-in for an enterprise domain.

Your role is decided by your organization
-----
With SSO, your CrowdVision role (administrator, staff, or standard) is derived from the group you belong to in your company directory. If your access looks wrong, contact your organization’s administrator.

entries.title: Dashboard
entries.content:
Dashboard

The Dashboard is your central hub for real-time monitoring. It opens to the Table view, a scannable overview of every monitored room in the selected building.



Figure 1 — The dashboard table view. Each row is a room; columns update live as sensors report.

-----

The table view

Each row is one room in the current building, and each column is a property or live measurement. A newly registered building starts with the room name and capacity columns; you can add, remove, reorder, and save columns. See Customizing the Data Table for the full guide.

-----

Occupancy status

CrowdVision derives an occupancy status from how full a room is relative to its capacity.

EmptyNormalCrowdedFullOvercrowdedStatus0%1% to 50%51% to 95%96% to 100%over 100% — needs immediate attentionOccupancy

-----

Choosing a building

If you manage several buildings, switch between them from the selector at the top of the dashboard.

1. Open the building selector dropdown at the top of the view.
2. Pick a building you have access to.
3. The table refreshes instantly with that building’s rooms and live data.



Figure 2 — The building selector lists every building your account can access.

-----

Switching views and Focus Mode

A toggle at the top of the dashboard switches between List (the table) and Graph (the charts — see Charts & Simulator. A Fullscreen control expands the current view into a clean, distraction-free display for wall monitors.



Figure 3 — Switch between List and Graph, or enter Fullscreen (Focus Mode).

- Click Graph to see visual analytics; click List to return to the table.
- Click Fullscreen to fill the screen; press Esc or click the control again to exit.

entries.title: Customizing the Data Table
entries.content:
Customizing the Data Table

The dashboard table is a live, fully customizable view of every monitored room in the selected building. You control which columns appear, their order, and which sensor readings they show.

-----

Reading the table

Each row is one room. Each cell updates automatically as new readings arrive — no page reload needed. If a sensor is offline or not yet installed, its cell shows a placeholder (-- or 0) until data starts flowing.

-----

Entering edit mode

Click Edit in the table’s bottom control bar. This is available only if you have administrator rights for the current building’s domain.

In edit mode each column header gains controls:

Grip handle (on the left)✕ badge (top-right)▼ caret (next to the title)＋ button (far right of the header row)ControlDrag to reorder the column.Remove the column.Open the swap menu to replace the column.Add a new column.Purpose



Figure 1 — In edit mode, each header shows a grip handle, a ✕ to remove, a ▼ to swap, and a ＋ to add.

When you are done, click Save to keep your layout or Cancel to discard changes.

-----

Adding a column

1. Click Edit.
2. Click the ＋ at the right end of the header row. A panel slides open.
3. The panel shows every metric not already in your table, as chips.
4. Click a chip to append that column.
5. Click Save.



Figure 2 — The add-column panel lists every available metric as a chip.

Live data starts immediately
-----
As soon as a sensor column is saved, CrowdVision begins routing that data to your dashboard. If a sensor for that metric is active in the building, the new column populates with live readings right away.

-----

Removing a column

1. Enter edit mode.
2. Click the ✕ badge on the column header.
3. The column disappears from the preview. Click Save to apply.

-----

Swapping a column

To replace one column with a different metric without disturbing the others:

1. Enter edit mode.
2. Click the column header title (not the ✕ or grip). A dropdown appears.
3. The dropdown lists every metric not already shown (so you cannot create duplicates).
4. Click an entry to replace the column.
5. Click Save.



Figure 3 — Clicking a header title opens a menu of metrics you can swap in.

-----

Reordering columns

1. Enter edit mode.
2. Grab a column by its grip handle (or click and hold the header).
3. Drag left or right; the target position highlights.
4. Release to drop, then click Save.

Your layout is remembered
-----
Columns, their order, and your selected metrics are saved per building. The next time you open this building — even after a refresh or signing out and back in — the table loads exactly as you left it.

-----

Pagination and autoplay

When a building has many rooms, the table paginates.

- Use Prev and Next in the bottom bar to move between pages.
- Click Play to enable autoplay: the table advances every few seconds and loops back to the start — ideal for a wall monitor. Click Play again to stop.

entries.title: Charts & Simulator
entries.content:
Charts & Simulator

Switching the dashboard from List to Graph opens the visual analytics view: charts of historical trends, plus the built-in simulator for generating test data.



Figure 1 — The Graph view tracks occupancy and temperature over time for the selected building.

-----

The charts

Two charts summarise the building:

- Occupancy (people count) — traffic flow, peak hours, and how often rooms approach capacity.
- Temperature — climate trends against the configured maximums.

Both aggregate readings from across the building and refresh automatically for the selected time range.

Sum view
-----
When the aggregation mode is Sum, only the Occupancy chart is shown (and widened), because summing temperatures across rooms is not meaningful.

-----

Time range

The Range buttons control how far back the charts look.

1D1W1MRangeThe last 24 hours, hour by hour.The last 7 days, day by day.The last 30 days, day by day.Shows

Time slots with no data appear as zero.

-----

Aggregation mode

A building has many rooms, so each time slot combines them into one value. The Data buttons choose how.

avgsumminmaxModeAverage across all rooms — the typical state of the building.Total across all rooms — best for total people in the building.Lowest reading in the slot — spots unusually cold rooms.Highest reading in the slot — best for finding the hottest room.Meaning

-----

The simulator

CrowdVision includes a simulator that generates synthetic readings — useful for demos, testing, and trying overcrowding scenarios without physical sensors.

The simulator is a separate service
-----
The simulator runs independently from the app. An administrator or developer must start it and give you its URL before you can use these controls.

Configure the simulator URL

1. Click the gear icon next to the simulator button.
2. Enter the Simulator URL (where the simulator is running, e.g. http://localhost:3001).
3. Click Save. The URL is remembered per browser.

Start and stop

1. Click Start simulator. It turns green with an animated dot when active.
2. Every 10 seconds it sends people-count and temperature readings for each room in the selected building.
3. After a few seconds, charts begin to fill — 1D range with avg mode is the best live view.
4. Click the button again (Stop simulator) to halt.



Figure 2 — The simulator button (with its gear settings) starts and stops synthetic data for the selected building.

Per-building status
-----
The button reflects the simulator status for the building you are viewing. If you start it for Building A and switch to Building B, the button shows B’s state — A keeps running in the background.

The simulator generates, per room every 10 seconds, a people count of 0–50 and a temperature of 18.0–30.0 C.

-----

Alerts from sensor data

As readings arrive — simulated or real — CrowdVision checks each against the room’s thresholds. If a room exceeds its capacity or maximum temperature, the notification bell turns red with a counter, a toast slides in, and (if enabled) a browser push is delivered even when the dashboard is closed. See Notifications.

entries.title: The Digital Twin
entries.content:
The Digital Twin

The Digital Twin view is an interactive 3D model of your buildings. You can fly around the model, inspect rooms, watch live occupancy and temperature, and — with the right permissions — edit the spatial configuration.



Figure 1 — The digital twin renders every room of the selected building in 3D.

-----

Moving around: the control panel

The control panel inside the viewer holds the navigation tools.

Reset ViewZoom In / OutPanorama ModeFocus on RoomTemperature ModeControlReturn the camera to its default position, centred on the building.Move the camera closer or further away.Auto-rotate smoothly around the building for a hands-free 360° overview.Isolate the selected room and move the camera to inspect it.Colour rooms by live temperature, from cool blues to warm reds.What it does



Figure 2 — The control panel: reset, zoom, panorama, focus, and temperature colouring.

-----

Left menu: buildings and floors

- Select Building — choose which building to view (the list reflects your domains).
- Floor Selection — show a single level, or All Floors for the whole structure.
- Register Building — administrators open the Register Building modal to upload a building’s JSON layout and set its name, thresholds, and per-room capacity before saving. The system assigns a unique id automatically. See Upload a Digital Twin.

-----

Right menu: rooms and details

- Search Room — find a room by name or id.
- Room List — a scrollable list of the rooms in view.
- Selection & Details — click a room (in the list or the 3D model) to highlight it and open its live readings.



Figure 3 — Selecting a room opens its details and live sensor readings.

-----

Editing rooms

Administrators and staff can keep the twin in sync with the real space. Select a room and choose Edit Room to configure:

Room NameRoom IdentifierCapacityMax Temp (°C)Theme ColorFieldThe display name (e.g. “Physics Lab”).The system id (read-only; set from the uploaded model).The maximum safe occupancy, which drives the occupancy statuses.The room’s upper temperature threshold; exceeding it can trigger alerts.The room’s default colour in standard 3D mode.Meaning



Figure 4 — Edit Room configures a room’s name, capacity, temperature limit, and colour.

-----

Editing building defaults

Authorized users can also edit a whole building via Edit Building:

- Building Name — update the display name.
- Global Max Temp (°C) — a default temperature limit for the building. A room without its own limit inherits this value.

Changes at the building level sync immediately across the 3D model and the dashboard analytics.

entries.title: Upload a Digital Twin
entries.content:
Upload a Digital Twin

A building is created by uploading a single JSON file that describes its rooms and 3D layout. This page covers that file format and how to upload it, then explains private domains.

-----

How to upload

1. Open the Digital Twin view.
2. In the left menu, click Register Building.
3. Select your .json file.
4. Review the name, thresholds, and per-room capacity, then save. The building appears in the 3D scene.

Alternatively, from the Administrator Panel, select a domain and use Upload Building Data (JSON) to attach the building to that domain at the same time.



Figure 1 — The Register Building modal: upload the JSON, then review name, thresholds, and capacities.

-----

The building JSON

A building cannot be built room by room in the UI; the whole structure is one file.

	{
	  "id": "building-main",
	  "name": "Main Campus Building",
	  "rooms": [
	    {
	      "id": "room-101",
	      "name": "Lecture Hall A",
	      "capacity": 120,
	      "color": "#4f86c6",
	      "position": { "x": 0, "y": 0, "z": 0 },
	      "dimensions": { "width": 20, "height": 4, "depth": 15 }
	    },
	    {
	      "id": "room-102",
	      "name": "Study Room B",
	      "capacity": 30,
	      "position": { "x": 25, "y": 0, "z": 0 },
	      "dimensions": { "width": 10, "height": 4, "depth": 8 }
	    }
	  ]
	}

Building fields

idnameroomsFieldstringstringarrayTypeYesNoYesRequiredStable, human-readable identifier (e.g. main-campus).Display name. Defaults to id if omitted.At least one room object.Description

Room fields

idnamecapacitypositiondimensionscolorFieldstringstringnumberobjectobjectstring (hex)TypeYesNoYesYesYesNoRequiredUnique room id within the building.Display name. Defaults to id.Maximum safe occupancy; drives the dashboard status indicators.{ x, y, z } — the room’s centre point in the 3D scene.{ width, height, depth } — the room’s size, same unit as position.Default room colour (e.g. #4f86c6).Description

Coordinate system

Rooms are placed by their position (centre) and dimensions (size). The origin { x: 0, y: 0, z: 0 } is the centre of the scene.

- x — left/right.
- y — floor level. Ground floor uses y: 0; upper floors use positive y spaced by floor height.
- z — front/back.

To place rooms side by side without overlap, offset x or z by at least width / 2 + neighbour_width / 2.

Thresholds are set after upload
-----
Temperature limits are not part of the JSON. Set them per room or per building after upload, through Edit Room / Edit Building (see The Digital Twin.

-----

Private domains

Every domain is either public or private:

- Public domains are discoverable — any signed-in user can find them in the Domains list and request to join.
- Private domains are hidden — they do not appear in the list and cannot be searched. Only invited users who know the domain can join. Private is the default.

Joining a private domain

- OTP invite — your administrator shares an invite QR code; you enter the six-digit code during registration (see Account Registration.
- SSO — for enterprise domains, you join through Single Sign-On, no OTP needed (see Enterprise Login (SSO).

Administrators: creating a private domain

When creating a domain in the Administrator Panel, leave Visible from outside off (the default). Share the invite QR code only with the people you want to onboard.

entries.title: Domains
entries.content:
Domains

A domain is an organization or environment you belong to. The Domains view lists every domain linked to your account and the role you hold in each.



Figure 1 — The Domains view: a control bar on top, your domains and roles below.

-----

The control bar

- Search — filter your domains by name.
- Enter Private Domain — request access to, or sign in to, a private domain.
- Create New Domain — open the domain creation wizard (administrators only).

-----

The domains table

The table lists the main domains and subdomains you are a member of, with the role you hold in each (for example administrator, staff, or standard). To configure a domain you administer, open the Administrator Panel.

entries.title: Domain Administration
entries.content:
Domain Administration

The Administrator Panel is the control centre for domain owners and staff. From here you configure your domains, upload buildings, and onboard new members.



Figure 1 — The Administrator Panel: your domains on the left, the selected domain’s token and QR codes on the right.

-----

Managing your domains

Your domains are listed on the left. From there you can:

- Add a new domain — create a main domain or a subdomain.
- Upload Building Data (JSON) — attach a building’s 3D layout to the selected domain (see Upload a Digital Twin.
- Enable Notifications — turn on alert delivery for the domain (see Notifications.

-----

Onboarding members with QR codes

CrowdVision onboards new members with time-based, one-time codes (OTP), generated per role.

1. Select a domain in your list to open its security details.
2. Find the token and QR codes on the right. There is a different QR code for each role you can grant.
3. Share the QR code for the role you want to assign. The new member scans it with an authenticator app and enters the six-digit code during registration to join with the correct role (see Account Registration.

Share the right code
-----
Each QR code maps to a specific role. Share the staff code with staff and the administrator code only with people who should administer the domain.

entries.title: Using the Assistant
entries.content:
Using the Assistant

The CrowdVision Assistant is a chat helper tucked into the corner of the app. Ask it questions
in plain language about your buildings, your rooms, current occupancy, or how CrowdVision
works, and it answers using your live data and the product documentation.

-----

Starting a chat

Open the chat widget from its button in the corner of the screen, type your question, and send
it. You must be logged in to send messages or access saved conversations. The assistant replies
in the same window.

Your conversations are saved, so you can:

- Come back later and pick up where you left off.
- Rename a conversation to find it again.
- Delete a conversation you no longer need.
- Start a new chat for a separate topic.

A new conversation is automatically named after your first question, so your history stays easy
to scan.

-----

What you can ask

Live building and room stateOccupancy versus capacityHow the platform worksConcepts and featuresAsk about“Which rooms are the most crowded right now?”“How full is the main hall compared to its capacity?”“How do I add a column to the dashboard table?”“What’s the difference between a domain admin and staff?”Example

When the answer comes from the documentation, the assistant includes the sources it used, so
you can check where the information came from.

-----

What it will not answer

The assistant is focused only on CrowdVision. If you ask it something outside that — general
knowledge, maths, coding help, translation, or other unrelated requests — it should politely say
it can only help with CrowdVision rather than answer. This is deliberate: it is an assistant for
your buildings and the platform, not a general-purpose chatbot.

Two more things to expect:

- It only sees what you can see. The assistant answers using the same buildings, rooms, and
	documents your account already has access to. It cannot reveal data from other organizations.
- It should say when it doesn’t know. If it can’t find an answer in the available data, it
	is instructed to say so instead of guessing. Verify important answers against the displayed
	sources and live dashboard data.

-----

Tips for better answers

- Be specific: name the building or room when you can.
- Ask one thing at a time for the clearest reply.
- If an answer looks off, rephrase — and use the cited sources to dig deeper.

For the data behind these answers, see the Dashboard.

entries.title: Notifications
entries.content:
Notifications

CrowdVision watches incoming sensor data for you and delivers alerts through two independent channels: an in-app feed for when you are using the app, and browser push for when you are away.

-----

The notification bell

The bell in the top navigation bar is your real-time alert feed. When an alert arrives while the app is open, the bell turns red and shows a count of unread alerts. Click it to see recent alerts, each with the event type, the affected building, and a timestamp.



Figure 1 — The notification bell shows a live count and, when opened, the recent alerts.

The bell also shows your connection state:

- Live — your browser is connected to the real-time stream.
- Offline — the connection dropped. Alerts that arrive while offline will not appear in this session’s list, though browser push (if enabled) is still delivered.

-----

Browser push notifications

Push notifications reach you even when the tab is closed or minimised — your operating system shows the alert like an email or messaging app would.

Enabling push

1. Open the Administrator Panel or your domain settings.
2. Find Enable Notifications for a domain.
3. Click it — your browser asks whether to allow notifications.
4. Click Allow.



Figure 2 — Your browser asks permission the first time you enable push for a domain.

Push registration is per browser: enable it on each device or browser you use.

Revoking permission
-----
You can revoke permission anytime in your browser’s site settings. The next alert sent to that device detects the change and cleans up the registration automatically.

Choosing per domain

Notification preferences are per domain — you can receive alerts for one organization and mute another. In the domain settings, toggle Temperature Alerts for any domain. The change saves immediately.

-----

What triggers an alert

- Temperature threshold exceeded — when a room’s temperature passes its configured maximum, an alert is dispatched. To avoid flooding, a five-minute cooldown applies per room: once an alert fires, that room will not alert again for five minutes even if it stays hot.
- Manual alert — an administrator can send an alert to everyone in a domain from the Administrator Panel. Manual alerts bypass the cooldown.

-----

Not receiving notifications?

- Browser permission — confirm CrowdVision is set to “Allow” (not “Block” or “Ask”) in your browser’s site settings.
- Cooldown active — temperature alerts wait five minutes between alerts for the same room.
- Opt-in status — confirm the domain has temperature alerts enabled in your preferences.
- Connection — if the bell shows Offline, the in-app feed is paused; reconnect to resume. Browser push is independent of this.
